metadata.title: Why a "count field" checked in one pass and used in another is a buffer overflow waiting between them
metadata.purpose: Two-phase parsing opens a temporal window: the second phase re-reads a field from the input assuming the first phase constrained it; when the phases don't share a validation point, the value flows through unbounded.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro am_onyx) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.prerequisites: memory safety, buffer overflow, parsing phases
metadata.exclusions: timing races (this is logic desync); use-after-free by delayed validation
metadata.visual_object: Two side-by-side code blocks, one arrow from input to the checked alloc (left), a separate arrow from input to the unchecked loop (right).
metadata.hook: A safety-critical length is validated in one phase and re-consumed unchecked in another — the overflow lives in the gap between them.

beats.narration_text: A WAV `smpl` chunk parser validates `sampleLoopCount` in its count-prediction phase and allocates a 512-item heap buffer; the later read phase reads the same field from the stream again and loops that many times with no re-check. Attacker sends count=2000.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: If you validate a field in an early pass, will a second pass always re-validate before using it — or do the phases drift until the check is silently forgotten?
A safety-critical length is validated in one phase and re-consumed unchecked in another — the overflow lives in the gap between them.

Two-phase parsing opens a temporal window: the second phase re-reads a field from the input assuming the first phase constrained it; when the phases don't share a validation point, the value flows through unbounded.
beats.visual: Two side-by-side code blocks, one arrow from input to the checked alloc (left), a separate arrow from input to the unchecked loop (right).
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: RIFF/WAV `smpl` chunk. Count-pass: `if (loop_count>512) error; alloc 512*16`. Read-pass loops `loop_count` times into 8192 bytes. Attacker sends `loop_count=2000` → 32000-byte write → heap overflow.

Two-phase parsing opens a temporal window: the second phase re-reads a field from the input assuming the first phase constrained it; when the phases don't share a validation point, the value flows through unbounded.
